here in San Francisco.

Well, it's, it's in the process of changing kind of even as we're, we're interviewing.
So I'll start with that caveat, but our managing editors are really going to be the heart of the content production and what GrowthX delivers for our clients.
So we are, you know, we're set up today as a services org, a marketing agency, but we are built on an internal platform that leverages AI workflows to generate content for clients.

So a managing editor is going to be someone who can work in that AI system, generate those workflows, and is technical enough to massage prompts, give feedback to the engineering team about what needs to change.
And I think the heart of that, it's less about like a coding background or anything of that sort.
But I think the technical aspect here is being able to communicate what makes content good and what needs
to change on the product side to improve the output of the content itself.

And so, you know, ideally somebody in this role has a background and has used AI a lot in their work to date, has some curiosity with that, but also is able to work on their, you know, is able to like generate and edit content themselves.
So, we're expecting that a managing editor will manage three clients, three to five clients, depending, but I think like two to three to start.
And we'll be generating five and editing five pieces of content per client.
The, the other members of the team and the pods that you'd be working with are an account strategist.
Who,
Who is kind of thinking about like SEO, content audits, the kind of like slightly longer term vision for delivering and relationship management, thinking a little bit more about the analytics side.
And then an account director who's really like the visionary for what we're delivering and the owner of the client relationship long term.

I think managing editors are going to be manipulating the content itself and generating it.

That sounds fine.

migrate some sites over from WordPress to Contentful.

And…
I'm happy to do that.
I've always worked well with technical people, despite not having a particularly strong coding background myself.
mean, I've taken a couple of classes.
I know some basic HTML, CSS stuff, but not anything I would write home about.
Um, yes.
Um, in terms of content engineering, I know I told Mariela I had just, um, recently completed a three-week cohort with AirOps.
So that's, I would say, my most positive experience with content engineering, other than I had a boss who would send me chat GPT written articles and be like, hey, can we post this?
And I'd be like, oh, okay, this is going to need, like, some help.
You obviously just sent, write an SEO-optimized article about this keyword.
So, I do have some experience with content engineering, making sure that it has that brand voice and overall.
All strategy makes sense and shipping it.
I would say that I do like to edit pretty closely to try to get that human touch in there more.

So generally, I've worked with human content editors or human content writers who, even if they use AI with their process, they've already vetted the content once to make sure that it's something that they feel comfortable owning up to.
And then I have definitely edited AI-generated content for multiple clients, both in-house and as a contractor.
So, you know, I have my own list of things that I look out for, words and phrases that I avoid, things that just scream, I was written by AI.
Tell me about some of those and how you avoid them and how you think about, like, preventing them.
Yeah, so definitely.
Basically, I have, you know, like my own list of prompts that I tend to go back to.
I would say first and foremost, my, the thing that I try to avoid most with AI written content is plagiarism.
So I always look for facts and I can use prompts to help me identify facts that might need to be substantiated.
Just doing some basic fact-checking, making sure that information is true and original, or if it's not original, it's cited to the original source.
Other red flags that I would look for.
I mean, just the absolute overuse of em dashes.
I know everyone says that and I love using them myself in writing.
So I know it's not an automatic AI issue.
But if there's like five per paragraph in many paragraphs, that stands out to me.
So break up the sentences, add in some semicolons.
And on the prompt engineering side, you know, make sure that I say very sentence length, perhaps give an example of how many words per sentence would be ideal.
I've never been able to successfully eradicate em dashes in AI writing, but as the systems advance, maybe that will be more possible.
Yeah, just looking for kind of themes that pop up in one or two places in an article, but don't make it through all the way.
AI has a way of sort of rambling and then losing the thread.
So looking for that sort of thing and make sure that it kind of wraps up, becomes full circle.
Um, can we talk a little bit about your air ops experience?
Of course.
And kind of what you, so we started with like growth.

was founded using AROPS workflows, and our Atlas system is very similar to AROPS, or like, you know, the concepts are the same, I wouldn't say it's very similar, but kind of like the baseline idea of building out a workflow that can execute multiple steps and have a human in the loop, that's definitely like the way that we operate.
Um, what kind of, would be curious what you took out of it, what, um, kind of how you think about AROPS as a tool, or that sort of workflow, maybe as a tool that you could leverage going forward?

Yeah, I mean, I love how detailed AROPS could get, how many steps you could include, including like, putting prompts in there, getting an output, and then doing something with that output to continue on.
Um, we, through, in the cohort, we went through a number of workflows that were made that we could tweak or edit to our specifications, so like, scanning.
Using drafts of articles to see where they differed in brand voice, and giving out a new article output, or just giving suggestions.
We all did our own, like, project capstone at the end.
And my experience in, like, career advising, I did something with resume writing, looking for various keywords, and identifying synonyms on a resume versus a job description.
Which is not, like, widely applicable at all, but that's how I sort of played with the tool.
I had some interesting things with using a pretty detailed, like, brand voice guide, and inputting different examples to get a similar output.
And I thought it went all right, based on an outline, or different inputs.
It's, it was interesting to see how, how tweaking things would change the.
The article that it would draft, and then different editing steps that it could help.

Yeah, that's a great question.
Definitely something that I have come up against.

I include, um, time estimates in my steps for myself to, uh, estimate how, how long a priority.
be, to uh,
A project will take and prioritize them accordingly.
I tend to be realistic with my clients about deadlines.

And most people are able to understand that.
I do offer rushed fees sometimes, but no one's taken me up on it yet.

But I've got price I'm pretty high because I do like sticking to my basic schedule, being able to framework out the project from an overhead view before diving into the smaller parts and just not overcommitting myself.
Yeah, yeah.
Okay, I mean, sometimes that is unavoidable, like, especially, you know, so how do you think about that?
Like when, yeah.
clear communication as soon as possible.
So if there's a chance that something is going to be.
I let people know, I definitely do sometimes work longer hours, or weekend evening hours, just to make sure that a project gets done, if that's what it takes.
Otherwise, I am a big fan of, I think it's called the Eisenhower Matrix, of like, if it's important and urgent, you do it yourself.
If it's important, but not urgent, you can try to automate it.
If it's not urgent, but important, you can try to delegate it, and anything else can just wait.
